answer:
**Manusmṛti chapter 5 and Viṣṇu Smṛti (primarily chapter 51, with chapter 80 on śrāddha offerings) share substantial overlap in regulating permitted and forbidden foods, including long lists of prohibited items (e.g., garlic, onions/leeks, village pigs/roosters, most fish, carnivorous/village birds, certain milks, and non-ritual meat) and qualified allowances for ritually consecrated meat in madhuparka, sacrifices, or ancestor/god rites.**[^1][^2][^3]

Both texts justify ritual killing (e.g., animals created for sacrifice; killing therein is "not killing") while condemning non-ritual meat as demonic, sinful, or leading to violent rebirths proportional to the animal's hairs—language nearly identical between Manusmṛti 5.36–41 and Viṣṇu Smṛti 51.59–65.[^4][^5]

**Manu's "vegetarian leaning" emerges more forcefully in two ways that exceed Viṣṇu's explicit allowances:**

1. **Blanket condemnation of fish:** Manu equates any fish consumption with eating *all* meat ("an eater of fish is an eater of all meat; therefore, one should avoid fish"), listing "all kinds of fish" among foods to avoid outright (despite narrow ritual exceptions for pathina, rohita, etc.).[^6] Viṣṇu imposes penances for most fish but excepts several types without penalty (pathīna, rohita, etc.) *and* permits fish meat in śrāddha to please ancestors for two months.[^7][^8]

2. **Generalized bird meat prohibition vs. Viṣṇu's sraddha allowances:** Manu bans broad categories of birds (carnivores, village-dwellers, web-footed/fish-eaters, beak-strikers, etc., including sparrow, crane, heron, parrot, etc.).[^9] Viṣṇu lists similar penances but explicitly allows unspecific "bird meat" in śrāddha for five months' ancestral satisfaction (alongside deer [three months], goat [six], ram [four], etc.).[^10][^11]

Manu lacks Viṣṇu's ch. 80-style inventory of pleasing meats (up to buffalo or wild ox), instead emphasizing ritual meat-eating as obligatory when appointed (abstaining then yields 21 animal rebirths) yet praising non-meat diets for purity, divine favor, and heavenly merit in its closing verses—tilting toward abstention without enumerating ritual meats.[^12][^13] Viṣṇu balances this with ch. 51's own strong abstention praise (e.g., meat avoidance equals 100 annual horse-sacrifices; "turn away from the eating of all meat") but undercuts it via ch. 80's permissions.[^14]

[^1]: Manusmṛti, ch. 5, [full chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/).

[^2]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, ch. 51, [full chapter](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/51/translation/).

[^3]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, ch. 5 (fines for animal-killing, no food rules), [full chapter](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/05/translation/).

[^4]: Manusmṛti 5.36–41, [passage](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-01ada6) (consecrated meat only; madhuparka/sacrifice/rites).

[^5]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 51.59–65, [passage](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/51/translation/#p-4a3c8c).

[^6]: Manusmṛti 5.14–15, [passage](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-75121c).

[^7]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 51.21, [paragraph](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/51/translation/#p-b0e8e4).

[^8]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 80.2, [paragraph](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/80/translation/#p-01d438).

[^9]: Manusmṛti 5.11–14, [passage](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-0cde46).

[^10]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 51.29/31, [passage](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/51/translation/#p-6f109c).

[^11]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 80.5, [paragraph](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/80/translation/#p-bd594b).

[^12]: Manusmṛti 5.35, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/#p-d256a2).

[^13]: Manusmṛti ch. 5 (post-5.41 vegetarian praise implied in structure; cf. wiki key passages), [full chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/).

[^14]: Viṣṇu Smṛti 51.71–76, [passage](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/51/translation/#p-ef774f).
